Article.ArticleTitle: RELAY: safety and efficacy of ramucirumab plus erlotinib in elderly Japanese patients with metastatic EGFR-mutated NSCLC.

Abstract.AbstractText: The global phase III RELAY trial demonstrated the efficacy of ramucirumab (RAM) plus erlotinib (ERL) in patients with untreated metastatic epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR)-mutated non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). We present safety and efficacy profiles of RAM+ERL in Japanese elderly (aged ≥ 75 years) patients in RELAY.

Patients were randomized (1:1) to RAM (10 mg/kg) or placebo (PL) intravenously every 2 weeks plus 150 mg/day ERL orally. Primary endpoint was progression-free survival (reported elsewhere). This report describes response rates, study drug exposure, dose adjustments, safety, and post-study treatment discontinuation therapies in Japanese elderly patients (RAM+ERL, n = 12; PL+ERL, n = 17).

Overall response rate was similar in RAM+ERL (83.3%) and PL+ERL (82.4%) arms. Median treatment duration of RAM and ERL was 6.0 and 15.4 months, respectively. Most patients had RAM and/or ERL dose adjustments. Adverse events, including grade ≥ 3 events, were similar in both treatment arms, although proteinuria occurred exclusively in the RAM+ERL arm (six patients; no grade ≥ 3 events). All patients received subsequent therapy after first-line study treatment; various subsequent therapies were used.
These results suggest that RAM+ERL may be a suitable first-line treatment option for elderly patients with EGFR-mutated NSCLC.

Eli Lilly Japan K.K., Kobe, Japan.

Eli Lilly Netherlands, Utrecht, The Netherlands.
Department of Medical Oncology, Kindai University Faculty of Medicine, Osaka, Japan.

OtherAbstract.AbstractText: Most people with lung cancer, especially in Japan, are elderly (aged 75 years or older). Elderly people are more likely to have side effects from cancer treatments, particularly chemotherapy. Newer cancer treatments, called “targeted therapies,” are thought to be effective and have fewer side effects, but not many studies have been done in elderly people. In the global RELAY study, people with advanced lung cancer caused by an EGFR gene mutation were treated with a combination of two drugs, ramucirumab (RAM) and erlotinib (ERL). This was the first cancer treatment the patients had for this advanced stage of cancer. The combination (RAM+ERL) resulted in better control of tumor growth and longer life compared with ERL treatment alone. In this paper, we report that the effect of RAM+ERL on tumor growth and lifespan in elderly Japanese patients from RELAY was similar to the effect in all Japanese patients, as well as all global patients, enrolled in the study. Side effects in elderly Japanese patients were also similar to the whole study group, although the dose of one or both of the drugs sometimes had to be reduced to help cope with a side effect. Most cancer treatments, particularly ERL, stop working after a while, so it is important to have different treatment options that can be used one after another. When RAM+ERL stopped working, most elderly Japanese patients were treated with other targeted therapies. This meant that the patients could live longer before needing chemotherapy.
